data.epi_content:
There is only one truly serious philosophical problem.

That is suicide.

To judge whether life is worth living or not is to answer the fundamental question of philosophy.

"Stop it. That lanky Frenchman wouldn't understand our language anyway."

I'm just talking about a book you read.

The passage I encountered in my mind was quite powerful.

"Then why don't you mention this part?"

Although it begins with a passage about suicide, it ultimately states that suicide is submission to the absurd while life is resistance to the absurd, that life must be lived, that life is worth living.

The paradoxical statement that even a repetitive, terrible, boring, or painful life must be lived.

"But the provocative first sentence is the essence of the book, rather than the hopeful parts that come later."

Sometimes my thoughts get scattered like this.

When what I want and what this young lady wants become slightly twisted.

I suppose this happens often when we want the same result but different processes.

It doesn't feel like we're completely separate entities, so it will quiet down soon.

Just occasionally. This happens when I become unstable.

If there were a mental hospital here, I would have given answers to get prescribed appropriate medication and diligently taken mood-stabilizing drugs.

Bipolar disorder, schizophrenia, mental dissociation—well, that's the same as schizophrenia—anyway, autism spectrum disorder, major affective disorder, dissociative disorder, neurosis, whatever.

This young lady is merely grumbling.

Saying she doesn't want to put blades to her body anymore.

But feeling one's life, rebellion, freedom, feeling it as much as possible—that is what living is, experiencing life to the fullest.

Therefore, I feel freedom to live, and I am doing what best demonstrates my existence in this world.

So put away those complaints.

I'm just saving myself.

Perhaps right now, I'm my own best doctor.

I get into the bathtub, run warm water, and take a shower.

This isn't a stupid attempt like last time when I filled the tub with water and tried to die.

If I just wash it away with water while showering, it's much simpler to handle.

Clean and not unsightly.

An arm with blood streaming down is utterly horrific, but an arm washed once with water, leaving only wounds, is somewhat bearable.

Thinking this, I lightly scratch again where I had scratched before showering. At first, blood seeps out slightly, but with a bit of stinging, it soon becomes clean.

Along a long line, beads of blood slowly emerge, then eventually flow down.

After staring at this sight for quite some time, I wash it away with warm water, then clean my body thoroughly and get dressed.

"Well, this much is fine. It's not like I'm seriously injured."

There's still a blood scent in the bathroom, or rather, the warm water has spread the blood scent throughout the room.

Well, opening the window should clear it out.

Talking to myself is perhaps creating a companion for myself.

In the past, when my father was still alive and well, he would always mutter to himself while reading news or books, so I asked him why he did that.

He answered that he used to have people to discuss these things with, but as he aged, he found himself alone in these interests.

So he became his own companion. I remember that odd conversation.

Why am I suddenly thinking about this?

Anyway, staying in my room without going out is safe.

I don't have any problems bothering me.

I don't have to pretend not to know Vivian if I accidentally run into her.

I don't need to exchange awkward smiles with Evan when our eyes meet, then pretend not to know each other.

I don't have to be bullied by Lydia.

There's no risk of encountering that annoying Crown Prince on the street.

Just sometimes when life feels so boring I might go crazy.
data.epi_content2:
When the first death and second death feel too painful, and I feel the contradictory emotion that I should still feel pain now.

Just coming to the bathroom, muttering like a mentally ill person, and making one cut with a blade ends everything.

It doesn't hurt much, and my thoughts become clear like now.

It's truly a kind of marking that represents rebellion against life's absurdity and a commitment to continue living.

I wonder if self-harm can really be packaged so grandly, but like most things, it's a matter of presentation.

If I jumped from this building and died right now, with good packaging it would become a terrible tragedy, but without packaging, meeting a powerful person without such intentions, it would just be a pessimistic fool's flying game.

"Ahaha."

It's not particularly funny, but strangely, laughter came out.

Since I'm in a good mood now, I should eat candy—pure sugar lumps—instead of chocolate.

Really, without any flavor or mixed ingredients, I take out a hardened lump of pressed sugar and put it in my mouth.

Then I drink a cup of coffee that has already gone cold after my shower.

With a feeling of my mouth being instantly refreshed, a sticky sweetness lingers along with the coffee aroma.

Actually, I finished all the chocolate long ago.

While enjoying this pleasure, I hear another knock at the door, though the maid should have left by now.

Knock. Knock.

Not the annoying knock of that impertinent little maid who knocks with her fist reversed, but a gentle tap with fingers, as if worried about disturbing the person inside.

And when I open the door, I see Vivian holding a bag full of cookies.

Is it now?

That's right.

Yes.

But it doesn't seem right.

"Um... Miss Erica. It's been a while."

"There's no need to call me 'miss' anymore.

Come in, I can't just leave you standing at the door."

"Ah! Yes!"

Vivian looks as if she didn't expect such a response.

Well, she probably thought she would be driven away.

Or that I wouldn't even open the door.

Come to think of it, I didn't think to check outside through the peephole in the door.

Well, it's not a problem.

It's not like last time when I had a pistol with bullets on top.

No, that can't be right.

The knife I just used for a bit of fun has been cleaned and placed back on the shelf as decoration.

Who would think someone would harm anyone with something so ornate?

"Would you prefer coffee, or shall I brew you a cup of tea?"

I can't offer chocolate.

I've already eaten it all instead of proper meals, three times a day.

Thanks to that, I'm starting to feel a bit hungry, which is good timing.

Unlike last time, I've been quite frugal with my food, so there's plenty left.

The coffee will be gone after about thirty more cups, but the tea is piled high.

"Tea! Please give me tea!"

"Just sit down for now."

Vivian enters the room and sits down.

But she's frowning slightly as if something is bothering her.

"Is there a problem?"

I brew her a simple cup of herbal tea and ask.
data.epi_content3:
"Slightly, um... it's rude to say after coming in, but..."

"You've already been rude enough, so it's fine."

"There's a smell of blood."

I thought the smell had completely dissipated.

Perhaps I've just become accustomed to it from staying in this space for so long.

Well, a suitable excuse comes to mind, so it shouldn't be a problem.

"It's a bit of a special... you know, that time of the month.

And do you really need to mention that out loud?"

The blood smell must be quite strong, vibrating through the air.

But well, if I insist, that's that.

If I say that's what it is, what can she do?

"It's not that important anyway. So why did you come to see me?"

"I heard you haven't been attending classes for a while, so I was... worried and came to check on you."

She drags out the part about being worried, as if she thinks I'll dislike it.

That's right, I do dislike it.

I really hate when someone pities and worries about me.

Yet the irony of wanting someone to care about me—what is this contradiction?

"Well, if you came because you were worried, I can hardly turn you away.

Oh, and I'm sorry for slapping you last time."

Vivian looks surprised at my apology, or perhaps beyond surprised.

Anyway, she fails to manage her expression and looks shocked.

"What? Did you think I wouldn't apologize?"

"...Yes, I mean, no, um... yes..."

"Anyway, I'm doing fine. I'll enjoy the cookies.

So now, please leave, Vivian. We're not the kind of people who share various concerns and comfort each other."

"Erica, weren't we friends?"

"No, we weren't. And we won't be in the future.

So, please leave quickly."

By sheer coincidence, the number of people I can talk to has decreased from two to one.

Having few relationships can be lonely, but the less you have, the less mental energy you need to invest.

Vivian seems hurt by my declaration that we're not friends. She sniffles a bit, then puts on a stern expression.

More precisely, it's an expression of someone who has made a decision.

"I'll leave today, but I'll come back again."

"I'd prefer if you didn't. Please leave, Vivian."

She leaves the room quite obediently.

She's quite stubborn, so she'll probably keep coming to see me.

Maybe I should escape to the classroom.

I'm almost out of food anyway.

Even the cookies Vivian gave me will be gone in two days.

But, Lydia...

Should I just ask Evan for help?

My head pulls me in that direction, but my heart screams that it's terrible—in other words, a thought I don't like.
